title: Why Output Size Matters for Architecture Design
concept: Output size isn't just arithmetic trivia — layers with mismatched sizes break the network, and unchecked shrinking can run a feature map down to nothing before you've stacked enough layers to learn anything useful.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does it mean when the output-size formula's division doesn't come out even?

options.text: The floor step drops the leftover fraction — a normal outcome, not an error
The network crashes immediately and the architecture must be redesigned
You must always add extra padding to force an exact fit

exercises.explain: Right — a leftover fraction is normal; floor handles it cleanly. Now let's see why getting this number right actually matters when you're designing a real network.

cards.title: The math has real stakes
cards.body: So far this has felt like pure arithmetic. Here's why it actually matters: get the output size wrong, or ignore it while stacking layers, and your network simply won't run — or worse, it'll quietly shrink itself into nothing.

cards.title: Mismatched sizes break the network
cards.body: Every layer's output becomes the next layer's input. If a layer expects, say, a 32x32 input but the previous layer actually output 31x31 because of an unaccounted stride or missing padding, the shapes don't line up — the network throws an error before it can even run, let alone train.

cards.title: Stacking bricks that don't line up
cards.body: Building layers is like stacking bricks: each brick has to be sized to sit cleanly on the one below it. Stack a brick that's the wrong width and the whole wall doesn't just look worse — it doesn't stand. A CNN with mismatched layer sizes doesn't 'run a bit worse'; it fails to build at all.

cards.title: Shrinking to nothing
cards.body: Say you stack layers, each using a 5x5 filter with stride 1 and no padding, starting from a 28x28 input. Each layer loses 4 pixels of size: 28 → 24 → 20 → 16 → 12 → 8 → 4. One more layer like that and you'd hit 0 — an empty feature map, with nothing left for any further layer to work on. Output-size math is exactly what tells you, in advance, how many layers you can stack before that happens.

cards.title: It's not just a training-time slowdown
cards.body: You might assume a badly-sized network just trains a little slower or a little less accurately. It's often worse than that: shape mismatches typically crash the program immediately with a dimension error, and a feature map that shrinks to zero size makes the network literally impossible to build, no training involved.

cards.title: No-Padding Stack vs. Same-Padding Stack
cards.body: Same six layers, very different fates:

left.text:
28→24→20→16→12→8→4
Shrinks every layer
Runs out of room fast

exercises.prompt: A network stacks a layer expecting a 16x16 input, but the previous layer actually output 15x15. What happens?

options.text: The shapes don't match, and the network fails to run — this is a real, common architecture bug
The network automatically resizes the mismatched layer to fit
Nothing — CNNs silently ignore small size mismatches
The extra pixel is silently duplicated to make up the difference

exercises.explain: Right — a shape mismatch like this raises a dimension error; nothing about it resolves itself automatically.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Stacking enough no-padding, stride-1 layers with filters bigger than 1x1 will eventually shrink a feature map down to nothing.
exercises.explain: True — every such layer loses a little size, and without padding to counteract it, that loss compounds until the feature map disappears.

exercises.sentence: Using 'same' padding keeps a layer's output size ___ to its input size, which is exactly what lets you stack many layers without running out of room.
exercises.explain: Equal — that's the entire point of same padding: hold the spatial size steady, layer after layer.

exercises.prompt: Which best explains why architects calculate output size in advance rather than just trying a network and seeing what happens?

options.text: So they know exactly how many layers they can stack, and with what padding, before a feature map shrinks to zero or shapes stop lining up
Because output size only matters after training already finishes
Because the formula is just a formality with no real effect on whether the network runs

exercises.explain: Exactly — computing output size ahead of time catches broken architectures before any code is even run.

exercises.prompt: You're designing an 8-layer CNN starting from a 16x16 input. Every layer uses a 3x3 filter, stride 1, and NO padding. Using the output-size formula, what happens by the time you reach the later layers — and why does this matter for architecture design?

options.text: By layer 7 the feature map has shrunk to 2x2; layer 8's 3x3 filter no longer even fits inside it, so the formula breaks down — this is exactly why architects run the size formula ahead of time, to know when to add padding or stop stacking
The network keeps shrinking forever without limit but always keeps working fine, layer after layer
The feature map size doesn't change no matter how many layers you stack, since 3x3 filters always preserve size
This is purely a cosmetic issue and has no effect on whether the network can actually be built or trained

exercises.explain: Right — every layer loses 2 pixels of size (3x3, stride 1, no padding): 16→14→12→10→8→6→4→2 after 7 layers. By layer 8 the 3x3 filter is bigger than the 2x2 feature map — it literally doesn't fit. That's exactly why output-size math isn't optional trivia: it tells you, before you ever run the code, how many layers you can stack and when you need padding to keep going. Next up: the capstone that ties this whole convolutions arc together.
